details.text:
الم

- الم Alif Laam Meem - These are from the Huroof Al-Muqatta’aat (الحُرُوفُ المُقَطَّعات), letters which are cut apart and separated from each other, with which some of the Suwar of the Qur’an begin with. The majority view is that none but Allah knows their meaning although some of the Sahabah and Scholars have provided some explanations for the meanings of these letters. It has been said that these letters represent a challenge to the Arabs in respect to the Qur’an having been revealed in their language and yet they are unable to reproduce the like of it. Another deduction is that these letters humble those who read them and put their intellect in their place whereby they realise that they are not able to understand everything.
Derivative: يَرُدُّو (يَرُدُّونَ) comes from رَدَّ – يَرُدُّ – رَدًّا and it means to return something to where it came from.

Derivative: يَعْتَصِم – Its origin is from عَصَمَ and from that there is اِعْتَصَمَ – يَعْتَصِمُ – اِعْتِصَام. عَصَمَ (‘Asama) means to stop or hold someone back to prevent them from a danger i.e., to protect. If you hold yourself back and protect yourself that is اِعْتِصَام (I’tisaam). It implies the meaning of holding onto something to protect yourself or prevent yourself from a danger and is comparable to the expression of “holding on (to something) for one’s dear life”.
Derivative: اتَّقُوا comes from اِتَّقََى (Ittaqaa) which literally means “to protect oneself” from وِقَايَة (Wiqayah) which is a protection. وَقَى – يَقِي means to protect someone else.

- اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ means you should protect yourself from Allah i.e., from falling into His displeasure through disobedience to Him. It practically means to take the precautions not to fall into His displeasure.

- “And do not (وَلَا) die (تَمُوتُنَّ)” – This is in the form of a command and تَمُوتُنَّ has نُون التَّوْكِيد (noon At-Tawkeed) attached to it which adds emphasis to the command and this نُون has a Shaddah (ّ) which is more powerful providing even greater emphasis.

- “And you are (وَأَنتُم) submitting (مُّسْلِمُونَ)” – أَسْلَمَ – يُسْلِمُ – إِسْلام is an “act” that manifests externally unlike آمَنَ – يُؤْمِنُ – إِيمان which is belief and internal. As such مُّسْلِمُونَ (Muslimoon) means those who are actively and practically engaged in submission i.e., obedience and servitude.

Derivative: تَفَرَّقُوا is origin is تَتَفَرَّقوا from the verb تَفَرَّق which is origin comes from فَرَقَ – فَرْق which means to separate and divide. تَتَفَرَّقوا means to separate into divisions and factions i.e., to become disunified.
Derivative: أَلَّفَ – تَأْلِيف when used with بَيْنَ (Bayna – Between) means to unify, combine and put or bring together something that is divided or broken.

Derivative: إِخْوَانًا (Ikhwaan) means “Brothers” and is more general than إِخْوَة (Ikhwah) which refers primarily to “brothers” by blood. إِخْوَانًا (Ikhwaan) is like “brothers in a cause”. إِخْوَة (Ikhwah) is used when the level of Iman is reached and the believers (Mu’minoon) become brothers (Ikhwah) (Refer to 49: 10) i.e., like blood brothers.
Derivative: أَصْبَحْتُم comes from أَصْبَحَ – يُصْبِحُ – إِصِباح which means to become or transform into something.

“So, you became (فَأَصْبَحْتُم) by His blessing/favour (بِنِعْمَتِهِ) brothers (إِخْوَانًا)” – Here بِنِعْمَتِهِ “By His favour” is brought forward in the sentence structure instead of being placed at the end of the statement. This draws attention to the word implying “especially” i.e., “So you became, especially by His favour, brothers”.
- “The rope of (حَبْلِ) Allah (اللَّهِ)” – The “rope of Allah” has been said to refer to the Qur’an, the Deen of Allah, the obedience of Allah.
Derivative: أُمَّةٌ (Ummah) refers to a nation that is unified by a cause.

Derivative: الْمَعْرُوفِ (Al-Ma’roof) is the اِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool) from the verb عَرَفَ – يَعْرِفُ and means “That which is known”. It refers to the good that is known i.e., knowledge of it is widespread. (Note: This is a linguistic explanation and not a Shar’iy definition of its meaning).

Derivative: الْمُنكَرِ (Al-Munkar) is the اِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool) from the verb أَنْكَرَ – يُنْكِرُ – إِنْكَارًا and means “That which is denied, renounced or not acknowledged and recognised”. It refers to that which is not recognised to be good or known to be bad. (Note: This is a linguistic explanation and not a Shar’iy definition of its meaning).

- “And there should be (وَلْتَكُن) from you (مِّنكُمْ) an Ummah (أُمَّةٌ)” – مِّنكُمْ (Minkum – From you) has been understood in two ways. The first is that it means that an Ummah should arise from you i.e., a separate entity from you (the believers). The مِن (Min- from) in this case is known as مِن (Min) for the purpose of تَبْعِيض (Partition or something from something). The second is that it means that “From you” you should become an Ummah that comes into being i.e., you as a collective become that Ummah. The مِن (Min- from) in this second case is known as مِن (Min) for the purpose of بَيان (explanation or elaboration).
Derivative: تَبْيَضُّ comes from the verb اِبْيَضَّ - يَبْيَضُّ – ابْيِضَاضًا which means to become white i.e., because of the light radiating upon them.
Derivative: وَتَسْوَدُّ comes from the verb اِسْوَدَّ – يَسْوَدُّ – اِسْوِدَاد which means to turn black i.e., because of the darkness upon them.
Derivative: أُخْرِجَتْ is the passive form of أَخْرَجَ which means to bring out. أُخْرِجَتْ means “Brought out”.

Derivative: يُوَلُّو comes from وَلَّى – يُوَلِّي – تَوْلِيَة which means “to turn”. It can be to turn towards with the meaning of “to show” i.e., they would show the backs to you in flight. Or to turn away with the meaning that they turn their backs in flight.

- يُوَلُّوكُمُ الْأَدْبَارَ “They would turn the backs to you or show you the backs” – Whenever the verb وَلَّى (to turn) and أَدْبَار (Backs) are used together, it carries the meaning of turning away in flight and not facing their enemy i.e., running from the battlefield and not having the courage to fight.

Derivative: الْمَسْكَنَةُ (Al-Maskanah) is said to come from two words مَسَكَ (Masaka) which means to be stuck and سَكَنَ (Sakana) which means to be still and stationary. The combined meanings provide the meaning of being stuck in a hopeless situation that you can not move from.
Derivative: يَعْتَدُونَ comes from the verb اِعْتَدَى – يَعْتَدِي – اِعْتِداء and means to aggress and transgress against lines or restrictions.
- بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ (Bi-Ghairi Haqqin) uses the indefinite form on the word حَقٍّ (Haqq) and as such implies the meaning of “without “any” right at all”.
Derivative: صِرٌّ refers to extreme or intense cold.
Derivative: حَرْثَ (Harth) refers to a crop that is ready to be harvested.

Derivative: أَهْلَكَ – هَلاك (Halaak) refers to someone’s or something’s final end, ruin and destruction. أَهْلَكَ (Ahlaka) – إِهْلاك (Ihlaak) means to bring about that final end, ruin and destruction upon something i.e., to completely destroy it.

Derivative: يَأْلُونَ comes from the verb أَلا – يَألُو means to neglect or fail to do something, or to refrain from doing it. With لَا the statement لَا يَألُو carries the meaning of “to stop at nothing” to do something i.e., to be relentless.
Derivative: بِطَانَةً (Bitaanah) refers to those who someone has a close relationship with, and can be confided in i.e., intimates.
Derivative: الْبَغْضَاءُ refers to extreme or intense hatred.

Derivative: تُحْشَرُونَ from حَشَرَ - حَشْر which means to be herded. حَشَرَ (Hashara) differs from جَمَعَ (Jama’a) which means to gather together in that حَشَرَ (Hashara) combines the meaning of being led with the meaning of being gathered. تُحْشَرُونَ means “You will be herded”.

Derivative: مُحِيطٌ comes from أَحَاطَ – إِحَاطَة and it means to encircle something like a fence. مُحِيطٌ is used to refer to Allah’s knowledge encompassing everything, that He is aware of everything and there is no escape from Him and His retribution.
Derivative: غَدَوْتَ is from غَدَا – يَغْدُو and means to go out or set off for a place in the morning.

Derivative: تُبَوِّئُ comes from بَوَّأَ – يُبَوِِّئُ – تَبْوِيئًا which means to settle someone in the exact position or place that you want to. It is used here to refer to positioning the army in their places.

Derivative: مَقَاعِدَ (Maqaa’id) is the plural of مَقْعَد (Maq’ad) which means a place to sit from قُعُود (Qu’ood) which means “sitting”. Here it refers to the positions which someone stays in for battle.
Derivative: هَمَّت (هَمَّ) in classical Arabic means to think about doing something without carrying it out. It can imply to be on the point or verge of doing something but not doing it.
Derivative: تَفْشَلَا comes from فَشِلَ which was used in classical Arabic to mean “to lose heart and courage”.

Derivative: أَذِلَّةٌ (Adhillah) is the plural of ذَلِيل (Dhaleel) which can mean someone who is powerless or lacking in power. Here it is reference to their comparable lack of power and weak position in terms of having few numbers and weaker weaponry.

Derivative: مُنزَلِينَ comes from أَنْزَلَ – يُنْزِلُ – إِنْزَال and مُنزَلِ (Munzal) is in the اِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool) form which means that they have been made to come down i.e., sent down.

Derivative: يُمِدَّ comes from أَمَدَّ – يُمِدُّ – إِمْدَاد. مَدَّ means to extend and stretch out i.e., to give more of something or to make something greater. It is used in respect to mean “to provide and supply or to give aid and help” whilst أَمَدَّ means to extend for something else and can carry the meaning of “to extend provision, help and reinforcement to someone”. “Reinforce” fits with the context here.
Derivative: فَوْر (Fawr) means immediately and carries the meaning of suddenly and quickly here. It can also imply from “its time” or “instant” i.e., “at the time of”.

Derivative: مُسَوِّمِينَ – مُسَوِّم (Musawwim) means to mark and was used to refer to horses that were marked for battle. It can refer here to the Angels having specific marks of distinction or that they are the riders of the marked horses or even that they will mark those they are going to target in the battle.
Derivative: طَرَفًا refers to a side or part of something. Here it means a part, section or flank (i.e., side) of the disbelievers’ army.

Derivative: يَكْبِتَ is from كَبَتَ – يَكْبِتُ – كَبْت which was used in the Arabic language to refer to closing the door on somebody and to turn them away in order to humiliate them. It can carry the meaning of humiliating someone with defeat. Its more general meaning is to suppress, put down, crush and subdue.

Derivative: خَائِبِينَ is from the word خَابَ – يَخِيبُ – خَيْبَة refers to not attaining what one wants or desires or not fulfilling one’s objective. خَائِبِينَ refers to their state in the case where they will be in a state of failure as losers after failing to attain what they wanted to achieve.
Derivative: رَّحِيمٌ is an اِسْمُ الصِّفَة (Ism us-Siffah – A noun of description or attribute) which implies that Allah is always and constantly merciful (Raheem).
Derivative: غَفُورٌ (Ghafoor) is an اِسْمُ المُبَالَغَة (Ism ul-Mubaalaghah - A noun that emphasises i.e., hyperbole). It implies that Allah is exceedingly forgiving (Ghafoor).

Derivative: أَضْعَافًا (Ad’aaf) is the plural of ضِعْف (Di’f) which means a multiple of something or doubling of its amount. أَضْعَافًا (Ad’aaf) means multiples i.e., an amount that is multiplied by many times or multiples. مُضَاعَفَة (Mudaa’afah) means to be multiplied on something. أَضْعَافًا مُّضَاعَفَة is an expression that means something that is multiplied many times over in terms of accumulation.
- أَضْعَافًا (Ad’aaf) is the plural of ضِعْف (Di’f) which means a multiple of something or doubling of its amount. أَضْعَافًا (Ad’aaf) means multiples i.e., an amount that is multiplied by many times or multiples. مُضَاعَفَة (Mudaa’afah) means to be multiplied on something. أَضْعَافًا مُّضَاعَفَة is an expression that means something that is multiplied many times over in terms of accumulation.
Derivative: أُعِدَّتْ is the passive form of أَعَدَّ – يُعِدُّ – إِعْدَاد and refers to the complete preparation of something to the point that it is ready to be presented.
Derivative: الْغَيْظَ (Al-Ghaizh) is the extreme anger or rage that makes your blood rush.

Derivative: الْكَاظِمِينَ is from the word كَظَمَ can mean to swallow and it can also mean to fill something to the top. The meanings are combined to refer to the act of swallowing or suppressing one’s anger when you are filled with it. كَاظِم (Kaazhim) is the one who is filled with anger but suppresses it.
- فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ is an expression meaning “In easy and hard times”.
Derivative: فَاحِشَةً (Faahishah) refers to a heinous indecent act and is used for acts like Zinaa (adultery/fornication) and what is related or similar to that.

Derivative: يُصِرُّوا comes from أَصَرَّ – يُصِرُّ – إِصْرَار and means “to insist or persist” upon something. صُرَّة (Surrah) is a bag that is tied up and from it the idea of not letting something go of something is derived.
Derivative: نِعْمَ is one of the verbs in Arabic called أَفْعَال التَّعَجُّب (Af’aal (verbs of) At-Ta’ajjub) and it is used to express amazement and how wonderful and excellent something is.

Derivative: بَيَانٌ (Bayaan) comes from بَانَ – يَبِينُ which means to make clear and evident. بَيَانٌ (Bayaan) refers to a speech, statement or communication that is extremely clear. A clarification that removes doubt.
Derivative: مَوْعِظَةٌ is an advice that penetrates deeply inside the heart.

Derivative: تَهِنُوا “Means to be weak”. It is from وَهَنَ – يَهِنُ – وَهْن and means to dilapidate, to grow weak and feeble i.e., from something that in origin should be strong and sound that then becomes weak.
Derivative: الْأَعْلَوْنَ is the plural of الأَعْلَى which is an اِسْم التَّفْضِيل (Ism ut-Tafdeel - superlative form) which means “the highest” or “superior” i.e. those who have the upper hand.
Derivative: الْقَنَاطِيرِ (Al-Qanaateer) is the plural of قِنْطار (Qintaar) and refers to a great amount of accumulated or piled up wealth.

- الْقَنَاطِيرِ الْمُقَنطَرَةِ literally means “Great amounts of accumulated wealth (الْقَنَاطِيرِ) which has been accumulated (الْمُقَنطَرَةِ)” and is an expression to refer to “Great accumulated riches”.

Derivative: نُدَاوِلُ comes from the verb دَاوَلَ – يُداوِلُ – مُداوَلَةً. Its origin is دَالَ – يَدُولُ – دَوْلَة which means to change, alternate and rotate in reference to time like the statement دَالَ الزَّمَن “The times have changed” which doesn’t mean that time has actually changed but rather it is an expression to mean that over time the situations and circumstances have changed. دَاوَلَ means “to cause and make the change or alternation” rather than just “to change and alternate” i.e., نُدَاوِلُ means “We cause the days to shift and alternate” between the people.

Derivative: قَرْحٌ (Qarh) is an injury and one of two words used in the Qur’an to refer to an injury. The other is جَرْح (Jarh) which refers to an injury that penetrates your body like a deep gash or wound from a sharp object or weapon. قَرْحٌ (Qarh) refers to a swelling like an insect bite or reaction to something. It is something that manifests on the skin but has not penetrated the body.

Derivative: يَمْسَسْ comes from مَسَّ – يَمَسُّ – مَسٌّ which means to touch. مَسٌّ (Mass) is used to describe touching which has a least amount of contact i.e., the touch of مَسٌّ (Mass) is lighter than other words in Arabic that describe touching or making contact.

Derivative: يَمْحَقَ is from مَحَقَ which in origin means to decrease something bit-by-bit until it ceases to exist. Its meaning is to bit-by-bit erase and eradicate something to leave no trace of it.

Derivative: يُمَحِّصَ is from مَحَّصَ – تَمْحِيص was used in Arabic with gold to mean “to purify” it by heating it with extreme heat to sift out its impurities. From this meaning it also carries the meaning of testing people and putting them on trial through the exposure to extreme circumstances so that the impurities are removed i.e., those who fail the test like the hypocrites for example.

Derivative: تَمَنَّوْنَ comes from the word form تَمَنَّى – يَتَمَنَّى – تَمَنِّي and is from the root letters of م ن ى and means “to wish for” something. It is usually used for something that is not attainable, not likely to be attained, or its consequences are unclear.

- “You turn back (انقَلَبْتُمْ) upon (عَلَىٰ) your heels (أَعْقَابِكُمْ)” – The Arabs say اِنْقَلَبَ عَلَى عَاقِبَيهِ “He turned about (or back) upon his heels” as an Arabic expression to mean to fall back and return i.e. to a previous state or condition.
Derivative: مُّؤَجَّلًا is the (Ism ul-Maf’ool) of أَجَّلَ – يُؤَجِّلُ – تَأْجِيل and refers to a “Deadline or appointed time (أَجَل) that has been set or declared”.

Derivative: ثَوَابَ – ثَابَ – يَثُوبُ means to return or come back to something like a state or condition. أَثَابَ – يُثِيبُ means to give compensation, to repay or reward. It is based on the conception that what someone did comes back to him in terms of recompense. ثَواب (Thawaab) comes from this meaning referring to “the payback or reward” i.e., that which is made to return and come back to someone.

Derivative: اسْتَكَانُوا comes from the root of كَانَ which means “to be”. اِسْتَكانَ – يَسْتَكِينُ – اِسْتِكَانَة means “to let things be”. It therefore carries the meaning of “to surrender and succumb”.
Derivative: رِبِّيُّونَ refers to those who are attached to their رَبّ (Rabb – Lord) or conscious of their Rabb.

Derivative: كَأَيِّن is an expression combining كَافُ التَّشْبِيه (Kaaf At-Tashbeeh – The Kaaf of forming similarity) and أَيّ الاِسْتِفْهًامِيَّة المُنَوَّنَة (Ayy Al-Istifhaamiyah Al-Munawwanah – The Ayy of questioning with a Nun attached to it). It meaning is “How many?” and is used to establish that something happened a lot.

- “And (وَ) Ridwaan (رِضْوَانٌ) from Allah (مِّنَ اللَّهِ)” – رِضْوَانٌ (Ridwaan) caries the meanings of contentment, satisfaction and pleasure and it is in the hyperbolised form indicating to greatness or abundance. “And Ridwaan from Allah” can mean that Allah grants you “contentment and satisfaction” in the case where you will be pleased and contented or that you will receive the “contentment or pleasure of Allah” in the case where He will be pleased and content with you.

Derivative: مَثْوَى is from ثَوَى which means to live and remain in a place for a very long time, generation after generation. مَثْوَى (Mathwaa) is the place that live or remain in for a very long time or ages upon ages.
Derivative: نُلْقِي is from أَلْقَى – يُلْقِي which means “to throw”. When followed by فِي (Fee – In) it carries the meaning of penetrating something and going inside of it.

Derivative: سُلْطَانًا (Sultaan) can mean a practical “authority” i.e., that which is in charge. And it can also refer to “an authority” which is used as a source of evidence or proof. Here it relates to a proof that came from above and was sent down i.e., revelation from Allah.
Derivative: الرُّعْبَ refers a fear that completely fills someone, the uppermost limits of fear i.e., terror and horror.

Derivative: فَشِلْتُمْ is from فَشِلَ and implies to become weak and to lose heart i.e., it is referring here to a weakness within themselves that manifested in their failure to keep order and obey the instructions that had been given to them.
Derivative: عَفَا means to “pardon” and implies that the act requiring the pardon was one that is punishable.
Derivative: تَنَازَعْتُمْ comes from تَنَازَعَ – تَنازُعًا which means to contend, contest and dispute with one another.

Derivative: تَحُسُّونَ is from حَسَّ which means “to kill” here. It is used to mean to uproot something from its roots. With an enemy army it means to kill them in a manner that wipes them out i.e., to kill them in a devastating manner.
Derivative: أَثَابَ (إِثَابَة) refers to that which is made or caused to return back and carries the meaning of to repay or recompense. It can be as a reward or as a punishment.

Derivative: أَصَابَ means to “hit the target” and is used with the meaning of “to afflict”. It implies that what you have been afflicted with was not an accident and that the affliction has hit its target.
Derivative: غَمًّا – غَمٌّ (Ghamm) combines the meanings of confusion and grief i.e., a state of being in distress or overwhelming grief and sadness.
- غَمًّا بِغَمٍّ means “One source of grief or distress followed by another source of grief or distress” or “One on top of the other”.

Derivative: أَمَنَةً (Amanah) – A Masdar (infinitives) that comes with a تَاء مِرْبُوطَة (Taa’ Marbootah – ة) attached to its end is used to imply “an instance” of something. For example, ضَرْب (Darb) refers to “striking” whilst ضَرْبَة (Darbah) means “a single strike”. Similarly, أَمَنَةً (Amanah) refers to a single instance of peace and security.
Derivative: بَرَزَ means to come face to face with something where it comes into view and appears. The meaning here has been interpreted to mean “to come out to”,
Derivative: نُّعَاسًا (Nu’aas) refers to the first stage of sleep, when drowsiness sets in and you drift off into a sleep. Then when you awaken you don’t know how long you were asleep for.

Derivative: مَضَاجِعِ is the plural of مَضْجَع (Madja’) which is a place where you lie down (on one’s side) and sleep. The meaning here is “resting place” i.e., the place that you will be laid to rest and die.
Derivative: اسْتَزَلَّ – زَلَّ – يَزِلُّ – زَلَّة means to slip. أَزَلَّ – يُزِلُّ - إِزْلَال means “to cause someone to slip”. اسْتَزَلَّ which means to try or seek to make someone slip.
Derivative: غُزًّى is the plural of غازي (Ghaaziy) which is someone who partakes in a Ghazwah (military expedition).
Derivative: حَسْرَةً (Hasrah) refers to a feeling of constant regret.

Derivative: تُحْشَرُونَ means “You will be gathered” but the use of the verb حَشَرَ (Hashara) implies an imposed and harsher form of gathering than the verb جَمَعَ (Jama’a) does. That is because حَشَرَ (Hashara) is used with the meaning of herding cattle together into a place.
Derivative: اعْفُ is the command form of عَفَا – يَعْفُو – عَفْو and it means “to pardon” in a manner of letting the matter go as if it had never happened.
Derivative: لِنتَ comes from لَانَ – يَلِينُ – لِين which means softness, gentleness, flexibility and lenience.
Derivative: شَاوِرْ is the command form from شَاوَرَ – يُشَاوِرُ – مُشَاوَرَة which means “to consult” others.

Derivative: انفَضُّوا is from the word اِنْفَضَّ. It is used to describe what happens when glass is dropped and it shatters scattering in every direction and cannot be brought back together again. انفَضُّوا is used here to mean they disperse away in a way that they would not be brought back.

Derivative: غَلِيظَ (Ghaleezh) is something that is tough. A heart that غَلِيظَ (Ghaleezh) is one that is hard and harsh and is difficult to penetrate. It is the opposite of having a soft and compassionate heart.
Derivative: عَزَمْتَ is from عَزَمَ which means to reach a firm decision about a matter and a resolution concerning it after having applied serious thought and deliberation to it.
Derivative: فَظًّا means blunt, mean and unapproachable.

Derivative: بِمَا – The مَا attached to the بِ here is known as مَا الزَّائِدَة (Maa Az-Zaa’idah) which is added for the sole purpose of emphasis. This adds to the meaning of the statement: فَبِمَا رَحْمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ to mean “By what incredible mercy especially from Allah”.
Derivative: يَخْذُلْ from خَذَلَ – يَخْذُلُ – خَذْل / خِذْلان which means to abandon or let someone down at the time of need.

Derivative: غَالِبَ is from the word غَلَبَ which means “to overcome or dominate” over someone or something and the imposition of one’s will or person over somebody else’s will or person. غَالِبَ (Ghaalib) someone who overpowers or overcomes another imposing himself or will over him.

Derivative: يَغُلَّ from غَلَّ has the Masdar (infinitive) of غِلٌّ (Ghill) and the Masdar of غُلٌّ (Ghull). If it returns to the meaning of غِلٌّ (Ghill) then it means to misappropriate or act dishonestly with funds and booty from war. If it returns to the meaning of غُلٌّ (Ghull) which means to have worldly aspirations and to exploit people for that purpose.
Derivative: مَأْوَى (Ma’waa) comes from the verb أَوَى which means “to seek shelter, refuge or protection”. مَأْوَى (Ma’waa) is the place of shelter and refuge.
Derivative: رِضْوَانَ is the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – hyperbolised) form of رِضا (Ridaa) and means “the extreme or great pleasure or contentment”.

Derivative: الْمَصِيرُ (Al-Maseer) comes from صَارَ – يَصِيرُ – صَيْرًا and it combines the meanings of returning and reaching an end or conclusion. الْمَصِيرُ (Al-Maseer) is an اِسْم الظَّرْف (Ism uzh-Zharf - Adverb of time or place). And as an adverb of place, it refers to the final place that one returns to and finally ends up in after having undertaken a lot of activity and work i.e., the final destination after life’s journey.
Derivative: سَخَط (Sakhat) is one of the words for “anger” and is used when a higher authority has anger drawn upon a lower authority.
Derivative: يُزَكِّي is from زَكَّى – يُزَكِّي – تَزْكِيَة which means to purify and implies alongside purification, to refine, fine tune and prepare for further development.
Derivative: مَنَّ (Manna) means to grant or bestow a great favour or blessing upon someone.
Derivative: ادْرَءُوا is the command plural form from دَرَأَ – يَدْرَأُ – دَرْءًا which means to ward something off, drive and push it away, repel and avert it.

- “And do not at all assume or ever consider …” the additional نُون (ن) with a شَدَّة (ّ) adds extra emphasis to the assumption. It implies the meaning of: “Don’t let that thought ever cross your mind”.

الصَّابِرِينَ وَالصَّادِقِينَ وَالْقَانِتِينَ وَالْمُنفِقِينَ وَالْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحَارِ

- The وَاو (Waw – وَ) between each of these words indicates that these qualities have been separated and as such each can represent a category of people in their own right rather than being combined together into one category. As such الصَّابِرِينَ (As-Saabireen -Those who are patient and steadfast) are a category, الصَّادِقِينَ (As-Saadiqeen – Those who are truthful and honest) are a category, الْقَانِتِينَ (Al-Qaaniteen – Those who are submissive to Allah and always ready and willing to obey) are a category, الْمُنفِقِينَ(Al-Munfiqeen – Those who spend i.e., for Allah) are a category andالْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحَارِ (Al-Mustaghfireen Bil As’haar– Those who seek Allah’s forgiveness in the early hours of the morning before dawn) are a category.
Derivative: يَلْحَقُوا comes from لَحِقَ which means “to join” and is used in relation to two things that are similar being joined together.
Derivative: فَرِحِينَ means they are in a state of فَرَح (Farah) which means “joy” and refers to a kind of joy that is the result of receiving a blessing.
Derivative: فَضْلِ means a favour and refers to something that is extra implying here something that is above or on top of what is deserved.
Derivative: يُضِيعُ is from أَضَاعَ – يُضِيعُ – إِضَاعَة means to be made to be lost or wasted.
Derivative: الْوَكِيلُ means the One who takes care of you and your affairs. The One to whom you leave your affairs to take care of them.
Derivative: حَظًّا (Hazhzh) means a share or portion of something that someone attains and is used for something that is good, fortunate and prosperous.
Derivative: مُّهِينٌ (Muheen) means “humiliation” and it comes from إِهَانَة (Ihaanah) which refers to a humiliation that happens publicly.

Derivative: نُمْلِي is from أَمْلَى – يُمْلِي – إِمْلاء – مَلَّا (Mallaa) was used with the meaning of to extend in the context of someone’s life. مَلًا refers to an open land that extends far into the distance. مَلِي (Maliy) refers to a long extended period of time. أَمْلَى (Amlaa) means to provide someone with an extension i.e., in time.
Derivative: يَزْدَادُوا is from اِزْدَادَ which means to increase in oneself. يَزْدَادُوا therefore means they increase themselves.
Derivative: يَمِيزَ is from مَازَ which means to distinguish or separate between things and is used to distinguish and separate the bad from the good.
Derivative: يَذَرَ means to let be or to leave alone.
Derivative: يُطْلِعَ from أَطْلَعَ – يُطْلِعُ – اِطْلاعًا which means to make somebody aware of something or a situation.
Derivative: الْخَبِيثَ (Al-Khabeeth) comes from خَبُثَ which means to become bad or rotten, implying that something was in a good state and then it became corrupted and impure.

Derivative: أُولُو (Uoloo) comes in Arabic as the Mudaaf (مُضاف) in an Idaafah (إِضَافَة) construction and means “Possessors of”. As such أُولُو الْعِلْمِ means “The possessors of knowledge” or “Those endowed with knowledge”.

- قَائِمًا بِالْقِسْطِ “Qaa’im with/by justice (بِالْقِسْطِ)” – قَائِمًا (Qaa’im) means to be “standing” but also to undertake or be committed to the undertaking of something. As such قَائِمًا بِالْقِسْطِ implies the One who stands by justice and sees that it is upheld and maintained.

Derivative: يَبْخَلُونَ comes from بَخِلَ – يَبْخَلُ – بُخْل which means to be tight, miserly and stingy with money in the case where you hold on to your money even when there are needs to be spent on.

Derivative: يُطَوَّقُونَ is in the passive form from طَوَّقَ – يُطَوِّقُ – تَطْوِيق which means to place a collar or necklace around the neck or anything that is round and placed around the neck like a rope that is used to hang someone. يُطَوَّقُونَ means “will be placed around the neck” implying that they will be hung and choked by the neck.
Derivative: فَقِيرٌ (Faqeer) is the one who is poor and destitute and cannot meet his basic needs.
Derivative: الْحَرِيقِ Al-Hareeq) is the type of fire that burns something completely where nothing remains.
Derivative: قُرْبَانٍ refers to an animal or something that is sacrificed to gain closeness to Allah as an act of worship. It is from the word قُرْب (Qurb) which means closeness.

Derivative: ذَائِقَةُ (Dhaa’iqah) is the اِسْم الفَاعِل (Active participle – Doer) of ذَاقَ – يَذُوقُ – ذَوْق which means to taste. ذَائِق (Dhaa’iq) is that which tastes and is in the feminine form here ذَائِقَةُ (Dhaa’iqah) as it is in reference to the نَفْس (Nafs) which is feminine.

Derivative: فَازَ means “He succeeded”. الفَوْز (Al-Fawz) the Masdar (infinitive) of فَازَ is to be saved from horrible failure and then enjoy success. الفَوْز (Al-Fawz) combines both being saved and then attaining success and its ultimate manifestation is to be saved from hellfire and being entered into Jannah.
Derivative: زُحْزِحَ is the passive of زَحْزَحَ which means to remove something or someone from somewhere and move them to a far distance from it.
Derivative: غُرُورِ (Ghuroor) is when you are deceived, taken advantage of, manipulated or conned due to your lack of attention or awareness.

- “Every (كُلُّ) Nafs (نَفْسٍ) is tasting/will taste (ذَائِقَةُ) death (الْمَوْتِ)” - ذَائِقَةُ (Dhaa’iqah) is a اِسْم الفَاعِل (Active participle – Doer) and is used to describe the state of someone implying permanence unlike the verb تَذُوقُ which would imply “will taste” death. The use of ذَائِقَةُ (Dhaa’iqah) here could therefore imply that every Nafs is already tasting death or that it is something that there is no escape from and is inevitable i.e., a permanent reality.

Derivative: لَتُبْلَوُنَّ is from بَلَا – يَبْلُو – بَلاء which means “to test” or “to put to the test and”. تُبْلَوْنَ is the passive form meaning “you will be tested”. The addition of the لام (لَ) at the beginning and the نُون with a شَدَّة (نَّ) at the end adds three levels of emphasis to the verb to imply something equivalent to the meaning of “You will absolutely most surely without doubt be tested”.

Derivative: عَزْمِ is from عَزَمَ which means to reach a decision or resolution after applying thought. عَزْمِ (‘Azm) means “resoluteness” in relation to maintaining Sabr and Taqwaa and can imply that resolve that aims high and does not get caught up with trivial matters.
Derivative: أَذًى is something that brings hurt or pain. It can be in the form of physical and non-physical abuse.

Derivative: لَتَسْمَعُنَّ - The addition of the لام (لَ) at the beginning and the نُون with a شَدَّة (نَّ) at the end adds three levels of emphasis to the verb to imply something equivalent to the meaning of “You will absolutely most surely without doubt hear”.
Derivative: بِئْسَ is used in Arabic for the purpose of dispraise and to express that something is bad. بِئْسَ ما can carry the meaning of “How bad, wretched or terrible” something is!

Derivative: مَفَازَةٍ (Mafaazah) is the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – More powerful and hyperbolised) form of فَوْز (Fawz) which means to succeed by escaping a difficulty and ending up in a good situation.

Derivative: أَتَوا comes from أَتَى which means to “come” or to “bring”. It also means to “do” or “carry out” i.e., what they have come with or brought in terms of acts. The meaning here of أَتَوا here refers to what “they have done”.

Derivative: الدِّينَ literally means the manner of how you deal with people and covers all interactions and relationships that exist in life amongst people. There is a famous Arabic expression كَمَا تَدِينُ تُدَانُ (Kamaa Tadeenu Tudaanu) which means “Just as you deal (i.e., with others) you are dealt”.

“Those possessing (أُولِي) Al-Albaab (الْأَلْبَابِ)” أَلْبَاب (Albaab) is the plural of لُبٌّ (Lubb). لُبٌّ (Lubb) refers to the essence of something. It is used to refer to a mind that is free of blemishes or defects i.e., a pure and sound mind which is free from distractions which cloud the mind and is capable of thinking about higher matters.

Derivative: بَاطِلًا (Baatil) is from بُطْل (Butl) which refers to uselessness, futility and falsity. بَاطِلًا (Baatil) refers to that which has no purpose, is false and has been done in vain. It is the opposite of حَقّ (Haqq).
Derivative: يَتَفَكَّرُونَ is from تَفَكَّرَ – يَتَفَكَّرُ – تَفَكُّرًا which means to think deeply about something.
Derivative: أَخْزَيْتَ is from أَخْزَى – يُخْزِي – إِخْزاء means to humiliate and shame somebody by exposing their wrong deed openly before others.
Derivative: الْأَبْرَارِ (Al-Abraar) is the plural of بَارٌّ (Baarr) which refers to someone who is righteous and is very obedient to his Creator.

Derivative: مُنَادِيًا (Munaadiy) is the Ism ul-Faa’il (active participle – Doer) from نَادَى – يُنادِي – مُنَاداة which means to make a نِداء (Nidaa’). نِداء (Nidaa’) is a call that carries over a distance. مُنَادِيًا (Munaadiy) is the one who makes a call or calls out (a caller).

Derivative: تَقَلُّبُ comes from تَقَلَّبَ – يَتَقَلَّبُ – تَقَلُّبًا means to move around in a back and forth constant movement. When used in conjunction with the land التَّقَلُّب فِي الأَرْض it refers to being free to move around the land, dominate it and prosper from it.
Derivative: مَأْوَى is from أَوَى which means to seek refuge and shelter. مَأْوَى (Ma’waa) is a place of shelter and refuge.
Derivative: الْمِهَادُ (Al-Meehaad) which is a place of rest and comfort like the cradle of a mother for her child.

Derivative: نُزُلًا (Nuzul) refers to what is prepared to honour the guest and provide hospitality to him in terms of food and drink. It comes from نَزَلَ which means to come down. نُزُلًا (Nuzul) is that first thing that is put down before the guest as an appetizer.

Derivative: الْحِسَابِ (The account) comes from حَاسَبَ – يُحَاسِبُ – حِسَاب / مُحَاسَبَة which means “to account” or “to hold to account”. الْحِسَابِ (The account) refers to all deeds which will be accounted for or held to account for.

Derivative: حَاجُّو is from the verb حَاجَّ – يحاجّ – مُحاجَّة / حِجَاج means to establish the حُجَّة (proof) and evidence to affirm the correctness of something i.e., to put forward an argument to prove a point.

- “I submitted (أَسْلَمْتُ) my face (وَجْهِيَ) to Allah (لِلَّهِ) and whoever (وَمَنِ) follows me (اتَّبَعَنِ)” – By adding “my face” (وَجْهِيَ) to the statement “I submitted” (أَسْلَمْتُ) it is an expression of humility attached to the Messenger ﷺ and similarly by omitting the ي (Yaa’) at the end of اتَّبَعَنِ (follows me) which indicates “me” this is a further expression of his humility in the case where he speaks less of himself.
Derivative: اصْبِرُوا is the plural command form from صَبَرَ – يَصْبِرُ – صَبْرًا and means to stay constant, patient, persevere and remain patient steadfast.
Derivative: رَابِطُوا is the plural command form from رَابَطَ – يُرَابِطُ – مُرَابَطَةً which implies the meaning of being alert, ready and on standby.
Derivative: صَابِرُوا is the command form from صَابَرَ – يُصَابِرُ - مُصَابَرَةً which means to compete and vie in patience, perseverance and endurance.
Derivative: تُفْلِحُونَ is from أَفْلَحَ – يُفْلِحُ – إِفْلَاح which means “to be successful” and is used in the Qur’an to refer to the success of the hereafter.

نَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ الَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْقِسْطِ مِنَ النَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

- Previous verses related to killing of Prophets was related to Bani Isra’eel and to the past. This verse addresses in its language the present and future and it is more general and not specific to Bani Isra’eel just as it condemns the killing of those who command justice and not just the Prophets. This generality, difference and the fact that it is speaking about the present and future instead of the past is further highlighted by the statement بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ (Bi-Ghairi Haqqin – Without any right) instead of بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ (Bi-Ghair il-Haqq – Without right) in the case where الْحَقِّ (Al-Haqq – The right) implies that the right was known whilst حَقٍّ (Haqqin – A right) implies “any right” which is not yet known i.e., any imaginable justification.

- “Then give them tidings (فَبَشِّرْهُم) of a painful punishment (بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ)” – The word بَشَّرَ means to give tidings and is usually used for good news. The use of this expression implies sarcasm like when somebody says to someone who failed in something due to it being his own fault or based on his own bad decisions: “Well done! Look at what you achieved!”

Derivative: مُّعْرِضُونَ is from the word أَعْرَضَ – إِعْرَاض and it means to be averse and to turn away from something or someone, not paying any regard or attention to it and ignoring it. مُّعْرِضُونَ (Mu’ridoon) are those that engage in that.
Derivative: نَصِيبًا (Naseeb) refers to a “portion” or “share” of something that you can benefit from.

Derivative: فَرِيقٌ (Fareeq) comes from the word فَرْق (Farq) which means “difference” and the verb فَرَقَ which means to divide and separate. A فَرِيقٌ (Fareeq) is a faction that has separated itself and wants to be different. A جَماعَة (Jamaa’ah) is a group that comes together as it comes from the word جَمَعَ which means to be gathered together whilst a فَرِيقٌ (Fareeq) is formed in the opposite manner by separating itself from the wider group. A جَماعَة (Jamaa’ah) is therefore formed by unifying whilst a فَرِيقٌ (Fareeq) is formed by division.
Derivative: يَفْتَرُونَ comes from the word اِفْتَرَى – يَفْتَرِي – اِفْتِرَاء which means to invent or make something up i.e., a falsification or fabrication.
Derivative: غَرَّ (Gharra) means to deceive and delude.

Derivative: وُفِّيَتْ comes from the word وَفَّى – يُوَفِّي – تَوْفِيَة which means to give somebody something fully or to give a person what is due to him in full. وُفِّيَتْ (Wuffiyat) which is in the passive form means “paid in full”. It is also in the past tense and in Arabic it can be used to talk about the future to add the implication of certainty to it.
Derivative: تُذِلُّ is from أَذَلَّ – يُذِلُّ – إذْلَال means to humiliate, lower and degrade someone. It is as a result of being overpowered and being put into a position of weakness.
Derivative: تَنزِعُ from the word نَزَعَ which means to pull something out of a place where it was comfortable and stationary i.e., to yank, tear or pull out, or to strip and pull off.
Derivative: تُعِزُّ from أَعَزَّ – يُعِزُّ – إِعْزاز which means to be given honour or a standing of authority with respect attached to it.

Derivative: اللَّهُمَّ means “O Allah”. It consists of الله (Allah) with a مِيم (Meem) with a شَدَّة (Shadda) attached to its end which is used in Arabic to “Call” somebody. Another example of this is the word هَلُمَّ (Halumma).

- “The owner (مَالِكَ) of the kingdom, dominion or authority (الْمُلْكِ)” – This phrase encapsulates and combines the meanings of ownership and kingdom together in one statement from the same root verb of مَلَكَ (Malaka).

Derivative: تُولِجُ from أَوْلَجَ – يُولِجُ – إِيلَاج – Which means to make something enter into something else. It can imply more force and deeper penetration than the word أَدْخَلَ which also means to make something enter something else. It can therefore imply thrusting something into something.

Derivative: الْمَصِيرُ is from صَار – يَصِيرُ – صَيْرًا which means to come back or proceed to some place eventually. الْمَصِيرُ is the اسْم الظَّرْف (Ism uzh-Zharf - Adverb) of صَارَ (Saara) indicating to the place that you eventually return or proceed to i.e., the final destination of return.

Derivative: يُحَذِّرُ is from حَذَّرَ – تَحْذيرًا which comes from حَذَرٌ (Hadhar) which is something that you should be cautious or alert about, like a fear of something impending. حَذَّرَ (Hadhdhara) means to instil in someone the caution and alertness about an impending threat i.e., to warn.

- “unless (إِلَّا) (it is) that (أَن) you fear (or are wary) (تَتَّقُوا) from them (مِنْهُمْ) of a fear or source of being cautious (تُقَاةً)”. It has been said to mean more simply: “Unless you fear something fearful from them”.

Derivative: صُدُورِ (Sudoor) is the plural of صَدْر (Sadr) which means chest. It is said that Sadr (chest) refers to a place unlike قَلْب (Qalb – Heart) which refers to a thing. The صَدْر (Sadr) is also larger than the heart and as such its usage can imply that a lot more can be contained within it.
Derivative: نَزَّلَ – تَنْزِيل (Tanzeel) means to reveal and implies a revelation that takes place over time and not a revelation that comes in one go like the form أَنْزَلَ implies.
Derivative: أَنزَلَ means “He revealed” and the verb form implies that the revelation was in one go and not over time.
Derivative: بِالْحَقِّ has been understood to mean “in truth”, “with the truth” and “with purpose”.
- Literally means: “Between your two hands” and is an Arabic expression to mean “in front of or before you”. Here it refers to what came before.

- “He revealed (نَزَّلَ) upon you (عَلَيْكَ)” – The use of عَلَى (‘Alaa – upon) in عَلَيْكَ adds the implication of “responsibility” whilst when إِلَى (Ilaa - to) is used with one of the forms to reveal, it implies ‘privilege”. نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ therefore implies “That He burdened you bit by bit” encapsulating the meaning of responsibility implied by عَلَى (‘Alaa – upon) and the meaning of being revealed over time and not in one go implied by the verb form نَزَّلَ (Nazzala – To reveal).
Derivative: تَوَدُّ is from وَدَّ – يَوَدُّ – وُدًّا which means to love something more than anything else.

Derivative: أَمَدًا (Amad) means distance but is usually used to refer to time and not space. The largest possible unit of time in Arabic is أَمَدًا (Amad) and it refers to the furthest possibly imaginable distance in terms of the time that it would take to reach something.

Derivative: مُّحْضَرًا comes from the word أَحْضَرَ – يُحْضِرُ – إِحْضَار which is commonly understood to mean to bring something. It comes from حُضُور (Hudoor) which means “to be present” and أَحْضَرَ (Ahdara) means to make something be present. مُّحْضَرًا (Muhdar) means to “be made to be present”.

Derivative: رَءُوفٌ (Ra’oof) – رَأْفَة (Ra’fah) is compassion i.e., when you see someone suffering and you have the urge to help them. رَءُوفٌ (Ra’oof) means incredibly compassionate. It implies understanding what someone is going through and having compassion for him.

- “What(ever) (مَا) it has done (عَمِلَتْ) of (مِنْ) good (خَيْرٍ)” – The use of مَا (Maa – What)) refers to something that is unknown or unidentified in contrast to الّذِي (Alladhee – What/which) is used for something known. This implies the good which someone may or not be aware of that he has done but Allah knows. The use of مِنْ (Min - from/of) with خَيْر (Khair - good) produces the meaning of “any good”.

- “And what(ever) (وَمَا) it has done (عَمِلَتْ) of (مِنْ) evil/bad (سُوءٍ)” – The use of مَا (Maa – What)) refers to something that is unknown or unidentified in contrast to الّذِي (Alladhee – What/which) is used for something known. This implies the evil/bad which someone may or not be aware of that he has done but Allah knows. The use of مِنْ (Min - from/of) with سُوءٍ (Soo’ – evil/bad) produces the meaning of “any bad/evil”.
Derivative: اتَّبِعُو is the plural command form from اِتَّبَعَ – اِتِّبَاعًا which means “to follow”. It implies a close following like following the same steps as the one who is being followed.

Derivative: أَطِيعُوا comes from أَطَاعَ – يُطِيعُ – إِطاعَة which means “to obey”. Its root letters are طوع and طَوْع (Taw’) literally means to volunteer. A such, طَاعَة (Taa’ah) and إِطاعَة (Itaa’ah) carry the implication of willing obedience.

Derivative: اصْطَفَىٰ means “to choose” and implies that the choice is exclusive to the one making it without any influence from anyone or anything else and also that the choice was made due to the purity found in that which is chosen.

Derivative: مُحَرَّرًا means to be “freed” and implies here to be freed from any other preoccupation of the Dunyaa (worldly life) and consequently to be dedicated fully and devoted to Allah’s worship.
Derivative: نَذَرْتُ is from نَذَرَ – نَذْر which means “to dedicate” something and to “make a vow or pledge” to do something.

- “The Imra’ah of ‘Imraan” – امْرَأَتُ or اِمْرَأَة (Imra’ah) can mean “woman” and “wife”. “The Imra’ah of ‘Imraan” can therefore mean “The wife of ‘Imran” in the case where the husband of Maryam’s (as) mother was called ‘Imraan. Or it could mean a woman from the family or lineage of ‘Imraan i.e., one of his descendants.
Derivative: أُعِيذُ comes from أَعَاذَ – يُعِيذُ – إِعَاذةً which means to place someone else in the protection or refuge.

Derivative: وَضَعَتْ from وَضَعَ means to put or place something down and is used in Arabic with the meaning of putting down or releasing a burden. In line with this meaning, it is also used to mean “to give birth” or “bear a child” which is its meaning here.

Derivative: الرَّجِيمِ comes from رَجْم (Rajm) which means “to stone” and was also used by the Arabs with the meaning “to curse”. رَجِيم (Rajeem) is in the فَعِيل (Fa’eel) form which is used with the meaning of the مَفْعُول (Maf’ool) form i.e., مَرْجُوم (Marjoom) which means “to be stoned or cursed”.

Derivative: الشَّيْطَانِ (Ash-Shaytan) – Shaytan (شَيْطان) has been said to be derived from the verb شَطَنَ (Shatana) which means to be distant and it has also been said to be derived from the verb شَاطَ (Shaata) which means to burn in rage.

- “I gave birth to her (وَضَعْتُهَا), a female (أُنثَىٰ)” – The use of هَا “her” followed by أُنثَىٰ “a female” adds to the emphasis of her expression through the repetition of the fact that it was a female that she had given birth to whilst she had hoped for a boy to fulfil her vow to Allah.

Derivative: كَفَّلَ – تَكْفِيل means to entrust or task someone with the full responsibility to take care of someone else. Here it means that Allah gave Zakariya (as) the responsibility of taking care of Maryam (as).

- أَنْبَتَ is used with plants and vegetation and means to make the plant or vegetation grow and to cultivate it to make sure that it grows healthily. أَنبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا can literally mean: “And He caused her to grow (أَنبَتَهَا) into a beautiful or good plant (نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا)”. This expression is being used here to mean that Allah made Maryam (as) grow and develop in a good manner. That could be in terms of her physical, spiritual and character growth and development. It has been said to mean that Allah made Maryam (as) be raised in a manner that made her very obedient to Allah and devout in His worship.
Derivative: هَبْ is the command form of وَهَبَ which means to gift.

Derivative: حَصُورًا (Hasoor) comes from حَصْر (Hasr) which carries the meaning of blocking, withholding and confining. حَصُورًا (Hasoor) is its hyperbolised form and means here that Yahya (as) will be somebody who holds his desires at bay and abstains from indulging in them i.e., incredible self-control.
Derivative: سَيِّدًا comes from سَادَ which means to prevail and implies that he will have the status of someone who is followed and known because of his qualities.
Derivative: أَنزَلَ means “He revealed” and the verb form implies that the revelation was in one go and not over time.
Derivative: الْفُرْقَانَ (Al-Furqaan) refers to that which distinguishes i.e., between guidance and misguidance, the truth and falsehood, the Halal and the Haram etc.

Derivative: غُلَامٌ (Ghulaam) can refer to a boy from the time of his birth until he becomes a young man, or to a boy at the years before he reaches puberty. It is also used in Arabic to refer to a servant.

Derivative: الْكِبَرُ (Al-Kibar) comes from كَبُرَ which means to become big and grow. كِبَر (Kibar) means bigness and the realisation of growth. It is used to refer to old age. Reaching الْكِبَرُ (Al-Kibar) means to reach old age.

Derivative: تُكَلِّمَ comes from كَلَّمَ (Kallama) which means to speak to people when you are you the one initiating the speech which differs from تَكَلَّمَ (Takallama) which implies speech that takes place between people on an even footing where you are speaking “with” someone as opposed to speaking “to” someone.
Derivative: اصْطَفَى means to choose someone based on their purity.
Derivative: اقْنُتِي from قَنَتَ – قُنُوت which means to be subservient, submissive, ready and willing to serve and obey.
Derivative: يَكْفُلُ means to take the responsibility to take care and charge of someone i.e., custodianship.
- “When they threw down or cast their pens” – This refers to the way that they cast lots to decide who would be given the responsibility of taking charge and care of Maryam (as).
Derivative: الْمُقَرَّبِينَ – “Those who will be brought close”. It is the Ism ul-Maf’ool (اِسْمُ المَفْعُول) form from قَرَّبَ which means to bring close.

Derivative: وَجِيهًا (Wajeehan) comes from وَجْهٌ (Wajh) which means face. وَجِيهًا (Wajeehan) means to be honoured and to possess a high standing and status amongst the people. Someone whose face is known and carries a status of honour.

- “‘Isa (عِيسَى) the son of (ابْنُ) Maryam (مَرْيَمَ)” – This is a unique construction in the case where someone is named as the son of his father and not the son of his mother. This naming is an expression of the uniqueness of ‘Isa (as) and his miraculous birth.
Derivative: الْمَهْدِ is a place that a baby is prepared for a baby to be comfortable and rest in i.e., a cradle. And here it refers to the age of a child before it is able to speak.
Derivative: Refers to the age when full maturity is reached and completed. It is after the age of being a young man (Shaab) is completed and progresses to what is known as middle-aged.

Derivative: وَلَدٌ (Walad) means “child” and comes from the verb وَلَدَ (Walada) which means to give birth or to have a child. وَلَدٌ (Walad) literally refers to “That which is born” i.e., a child from birth.
Derivative: بَشَرٌ (Bashar) means a mortal human being.

- “In that way (كَذَٰلِكِ) Allah (اللَّهُ) creates (يَخْلُقُ) what (مَا) He wills (يَشَاءُ)” – In Aayah 40 of this Surah in relation to Zakariya (as) having a child Allah stated: كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ “In that way (كَذَٰلِكِ) Allah (اللَّهُ) does (يَفْعَلُ) what (مَا) He wills (يَشَاءُ)”. The only difference is the choice of verb يَخْلُقُ (He creates) and يَفْعَلُ (He does). The significance of this is that the use of يَخْلُقُ (He creates) here in this context dispels and refutes all claims that were made in respect to the birth of ‘Isa (as) and the consequent false attributions made to Allah.

Derivative: أُبْرِئُ is from أَبْرَأَ – يُبْرِئُ – إِبْرَاء which in origin comes from بَرِئَ (Bari’a) which means be free or clear of something. أَبْرَأَ means to make something free or clear of something and is used to mean “to cure” or “to heal” i.e., to free or clear the sickness.

Derivative: تَدَّخِرُونَ comes from اِدَّخَرَ which in origin is اِذْتَكَرَ but the ذال (Dhaal) and تاء (Taa’) have been combined into دَال بِشَدَّة (A Daal with Shaddah - دّ) to make اِدَّخَرَ (Iddakara). ذَكِيرَة (Dhakeera) means “a treasure” or “provisions, stores, supplies etc”. اِدَّخَرَ (Iddakara) means to prepare or keep something back for the time that it is needed i.e., to stow or store away.

- بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ (Bayna Yadayya) - بَيْنَ يَدَى (Bayna Yadaa) - “Between the two hands” is an Arabic expression to mean “What is directly in front of (for place) or before (for time)”. بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ means that which “Was right before me” i.e., came before me or what “Is directly before and in front of me now”

Derivative: مَكَرُوا means “They plotted or schemed” from مَكَرَ (Makara) which means “He plotted”. It is used in respect to those plots that are hidden and involve conspiring, conniving and some slyness or underhandedness i.e., when deception is used in the plot.

Derivative: مُتَوَفِّي (Mutawaffiy) is from the verb تَوَفَّى (Tawaffaa). وَفَّى (Waffaa) means to give in full or to give someone’s due in full. تَوَفَّى (Tawaffaa) means to take in full. إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ (Inniy Mutawaffeeka) means “Verily, I am taking you away fully”. تَوَفَّى (Tawaffaa) is used in the Qur’an to refer to the Angels “taking away” the soul of a person at the time of death. Here Allah is saying “I am taking you away entirely”. Even though يُتَوَفَّى is used in Arabic to express the meaning that someone has died, it does not mean to die but rather it means “He has been taken away”. For تَوَفَّى (Tawaffaa) to carry the meaning of “to die” it must be qualified explicitly or implicitly by the mention of death alongside it, otherwise it means “to take in full or completely”.

Derivative: الْمُمْتَرِينَ comes from the root letters م ر ى and the form اِمْتَرَى. الْمُمْتَرِينَ is the plural of مُمْتَرٍ which is someone who has fallen in doubt مِرْيَة (Miryah – Doubt). It relates to the doubt that pushes someone to argue and dispute based on delusions and not facts.

Derivative: نَبْتَهِلْ is from اِبْتَهَلَ – يَبْتَهِلُ – اِبْتِهَال. أَبْهَلَ (Abhala) means to leave someone or something in its state to do as it wishes. For example, instead of arguing with someone you leave them be. اِبْتَهَلَ (Ibtahala) means to let Allah deal with an issue or matter of dispute whilst letting it be amongst yourselves. It involves supplicating to Allah.

Derivative: حَنِيفًا (Haneef) refers to someone who turns away from something and has an aversion to it. It implies here someone who turns away from all false beliefs and practices and being focused and devoted to the true beliefs and Deen.

Derivative: مُتَشَابِهَاتٌ (Mutashaabihaat) comes from تَشَابَهَ which means for things or matters “to resemble” each other. When things resemble each other, it is difficult to distinguish between them and as such the word مُتَشَابِه (Mutashaabih) is used to indicate to something which is ambiguous, obscure and unclear, in addition to the meaning of resembling each other.

Derivative: الرَّاسِخُونَ comes from رُسُوخ (Rusookh) which means to seep in and permeate, and to become firmly established and deeply rooted. الرَّاسِخُونَ are those who have absorbed knowledge and it has become deeply rooted in them.

Derivative: تَأْوِيلِ (Ta’weel) means “interpretation” and here it is referring to someone’s unique interpretation that he has come up with which is in line with his desires or which he uses for his own benefit.

- “They are (هُنَّ) the mother of (أُمُّ) the Book (أُمُّ)” – In reference to the Aayaat which are Muhkamaat (Clear and explicit in their indicated meaning) the form هُنَّ (They) is being used to refer to them instead of هَا (Haa) which is usually used in Arabic with non-Human things or those which do not possess a mind (‘Aql). هُنَّ which means “They in reference to female humans” is used here to magnify the glory of these Aayaat as if they have been promoted from هَا to هُنَّ.
- أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ (Ummu l-Kitaab) literally means “the mother (أُمُّ) of the Book (الْكِتَابِ)”. أُمُّ (Umm) which is in reference to the Aayaat which are Muhkamaat (Clear and explicit in their indicated meaning) means here the “foundation” or “Crux” of الْكِتَابِ (Al-Kitaab - The Book) in the case where they contain the pillars of the Deen, its rulings and prescribed punishments (Hudood) etc.
- الْكِتَابِ (Al-Kitaab) in addition to carrying the meaning of Al-Qur’an, it also carries the meaning of “The Law”. As such it can be understood that the Aayaat which are Muhkamaat (Clear and explicit in their indicated meaning) are the foundation or origin (أُمُّ - Umm) of Al-Kitaab (The law) i.e., the Sharee’ah rulings.

“Those who possess (أُولُو) sound and pure minds (الْأَلْبَابِ)”. أَلْبَاب (Albaab) is the plural of لُبّ (Lubb) which is the mind (‘Aql) that is free of blemishes, distractions and biases. It is the highest form of عَقْل (‘Aql -Mind).
- تُؤْمِنُوا comes from آمَنَ. When used with بِ it means “believe in” something. When used with لِ it means “to trust” or “to follow and obey”.
Derivative: يَخْتَصُّ from اِخْتَصَّ – يَخْتَصُّ – اِخْتِصَاصًا which means here to choose exclusively, single out or confer a favour upon specially.
Derivative: قِنطَار (Qintaar) is a huge pile of something and is used to refer to a huge pile of money i.e., gold and silver.

Derivative: خَلَاقَ (Khalaaq) means a portion or part of something. It comes from the word خَلَقَ (to create) and is said to refer to a single part of what is created that can benefit them. It is used to refer to a share or portion of goodness and happiness.
Derivative: بَشَرٍ (Bashar) refers to a mortal (human) being. It comes from the word بَشْرَة (Bashrah) which means the outer skin. Human beings are called بَشَرٍ (Bashar) because their skin shows.
Derivative: رَبَّانِيِّينَ (Rabbaaniyeen) are those who attached or connected to their Lord. Those who are most cognisant of their relationship with their Lord (Rabb).
Derivative: هَبْ (Hab) is the command form from the verb وَهَبَ (Wahaba) which means to gift.
Derivative: الْوَهَّابُ (Al-Wahhaab) – The One who constantly or continuously gives gift or grants.
Derivative: إِصْر (Isr) refers to a very strong and weighty agreement or covenant.
Derivative: مِيثَاقَ (Meethaaq) refers to a strong contract, covenant or agreement between parties, one in which each party fully understands the serious of what has been agreed to.

“You will surely without doubt believe (لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ) in him (بِهِ) and (وَ) you will surely without a doubt support (لَتَنصُرُنَّ) him (هُ)” – The verbs لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ and لَتَنصُرُنَّ have been provided with the greatest level of emphasis by attaching the لام (Laam لِ – Laam At-Tawkeed) to their beginning and by attaching the نُون with a شَدَّة (نَّ) to their end.
Derivative: يَبْغُونَ is from the word بَغَا – يَبْغُو and it means to pursue and seek something i.e., the object of your desire.

- The Qur’an uses two phrases: وَمَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا “And what was revealed upon (عَلَى) us” and وَمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا “And what was revealed to (إِلَى) us” – When عَلَى (On/upon) is used it implies a responsibility has been placed and something that needs to be carried and lived by. When إِلَى (to) is used it implies that something has been given and gifted. This Aayah can be compared with Aayah 136 of Surah Al-Baqarah in light of this difference and the two different audiences that are being addressed.

Derivative: يَبْتَغِ is from اِبْتَغَى – يَبْتَغِي – اِبْتَغَاء and carries the same meaning as بَغَا – يَبْغُو which is to pursue something as the object of one’s desire but the pattern form of اِبْتَغَى is اِفْتَعَلَ which implies more effort is placed in the action of the verb. As such اِبْتَغَى can be understood to mean “to seriously pursue” something.
Derivative: يُخَفَّفُ is from the word خّفَّفَ – يُخَفِّفُ – تَخْفِيف which means to lighten or make something lighter. يُخَفَّفُ is the passive form and means “to be lightened”.
Derivative: يُنظَرُونَ can mean “to be looked at” and also “to be given respite” i.e., a delay before something is imposed upon them.

Derivative: الْمِيعَادَ (Al-Mee’aad) comes from the meaning of وَعْد (Wa’d) which means promise. الْمِيعَادَ (Al-Mee’aad) caries the meanings of the time of the promise, the place of the promise and also the promise itself.
Derivative: يُخْلِفُ is from أَخْلَفَ which means to go back on something like one’s word or promise, or to break and violate it.

Derivative: حَنِيفًا (Haneefan) here is in the حَال (Haal) form which indicates to the manner of how something is done. It follows the statement “Follow the Millah of Ibrahim” and explains how it should be done: حَنِيفًا (Haneefan). It means that it should be done in a solely dedicated manner that inclines towards the truth and inclines away from all forms of falsehood.

Derivative: تَصُدُّونَ comes from صَدَّ – يَصُدُّ – صَدَّا which means to obstruct, divert and block. The verb is both transitive and intransitive meaning that it can be used with an object or without an object. As such, تَصُدُّونَ can linguistically imply that “you obstruct or avert yourselves” just as it can imply that “you obstruct or avert others”. In this Aayah its meaning is to obstruct or avert others in the case where the object of their obstruction is explicitly mentioned.
Derivative: عِوَجًا means crookedness in the case where something is meant to be straight and is then bent out of shape.
